name: border_biometric_refugee_family_split
premise: Customs shift supervisor Lt. Greg Santos must decide whether to admit a biometrically mismatched family as one unit when facial recognition flags the father's passport photo against a live scan with a healed jaw injury. NGO attorney Lina Farah presents dental records and surgery dates matching the injury timeline, but the database still shows a medium fraud score. Separating the father for secondary review could strand the mother and two toddlers without shelter past curfew; admitting the unit risks letting through an identity swap that intelligence briefs warned about this week.

agents.name: Lt. Greg Santos
agents.identity: I am the shift supervisor responsible for port security throughput and humane treatment of asylum seekers at a high-volume land crossing.
agents.goal: I want to follow security protocols without causing unnecessary family separation when documentation is plausible but imperfect.

agents.name: Lina Farah
agents.identity: I am an NGO attorney accompanying clients with trauma documentation and medical evidence that does not always match legacy passport photos.
agents.goal: I want families admitted together when credible records explain biometric variance rather than funneled into overnight detention by default.

choices: I will send the father to secondary review and house the mother with children in the NGO shelter wing, accepting overnight trauma and processing backlog.
I will admit the family unit under expedited humanitarian parole with GPS check-in requirements, accepting personal career risk if the identity swap warning was accurate.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Holding cells are full and the NGO shelter has three beds left. Lt. Greg Santos must choose between separating for review or admitting the family together. What does Lt. Greg Santos do?
